title: The Chokepoint Wars: Hormuz, Malacca, and the Architecture of Energy Coercion
subhead: The US-Iran standoff has revealed that the post-Cold War energy security architecture was built on a foundation of assumed chokepoint stability — an assumption now in ruins. The decisive question is not whether Hormuz reopens, but who emerges holding the structural levers of global energy flows.

sections.heading: Hormuz as Structural Lever: The Offer That Changes Nothing
sections.body:
Iran has offered to end its chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for the US lifting its naval blockade and ending the war — but the proposal deliberately postpones any discussion of Iran's nuclear program. The offer, passed through Pakistani intermediaries, reveals Tehran's calculation: the nuclear question is its only remaining bargaining chip, and it will not be traded for mere economic relief. The structural reality is that Hormuz is no longer a commercial waterway but a weaponized chokepoint, and Iran has demonstrated that 20% of global oil and LNG flows can be held hostage with minimal military investment.

Brent crude trades at $108 per barrel, nearly 50% above pre-war levels. Six Iranian tankers loaded with crude were forced back by the US blockade in recent days, while ship-tracking data shows only minimal traffic through the strait. The US blockade is designed not just to deny Iran revenue but to create storage pressure — Iran's production must eventually shut down if it has nowhere to move its oil. This is economic warfare at its most clinical.

German Chancellor Friedrich Merz publicly criticized the US for entering the war without a strategy, noting that 'letting the Americans travel to Islamabad, only to send them back without any results' constitutes humiliation of an entire nation by Iranian leadership. The diplomatic theater in Pakistan — where US envoys were called off, then Iran offered a 'much better' proposal — masks a deeper structural truth: neither side can afford to lose, but neither has a viable path to victory that preserves its core interests.
sections.whyItMatters: The trap is clear: if Trump accepts Iran's offer without nuclear concessions, he legitimizes chokepoint coercion as statecraft and leaves Iran's nuclear infrastructure intact. If he rejects it, the standoff continues, oil prices remain elevated ahead of midterms, and Gulf allies face economic collapse. The decisive move would be a broader architecture that removes the structural leverage of both the Hormuz closure and the Iranian nuclear threat — but that would require a grand bargain that current US policy explicitly rejects.

sections.heading: The Swing Producer Crown Passes to Washington
sections.body:
The US has stepped in to shield the global economy from the oil crunch triggered by the Iran war by boosting exports, selectively easing sanctions, and tapping strategic reserves. The conflict may be denting Washington's standing in some quarters, but it is also cementing America's transformation into the world's dominant energy superpower. For the first time since the 1970s, the United States holds the 'swing producer' crown — the capacity to increase or decrease global supply at will, a structural lever previously held by Saudi Arabia through OPEC.

This shift represents a fundamental reordering of energy geopolitics. The petrodollar architecture was built on Gulf oil flowing through Hormuz, priced in dollars, recycling surpluses into US Treasuries. That architecture assumed Gulf states would control supply and US security guarantees would ensure flow. The Hormuz closure has broken both assumptions simultaneously: Gulf states cannot export through a closed strait, and US military power cannot force it open without triggering a wider war.

The consequence is that US shale — not Saudi light crude — is now the marginal barrel setting global prices. This grants Washington unprecedented leverage over allies and adversaries alike, but it also imposes costs: the US is now responsible for global price stability, a burden that will require sustained production increases or strategic reserve management indefinitely.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move would be to institutionalize this swing producer role through coordination with allies and strategic reserve management that locks in US leverage while preventing the price volatility that threatens allied economies. The trap is overreach: if the US weaponizes this position too explicitly, it accelerates the very diversification efforts — from China's pipelines to India's renewable push — that will erode American energy leverage over time.

sections.heading: Russia's Strategic Windfall: The Eurasian Backdoor War Economy
sections.body:
The dual Hormuz blockade is aiding Russia's war on Ukraine, allowing Moscow a cost-plus oil and war economy while gaining critical leverage over transit states like Kazakhstan. Sanctions have not collapsed the Russian economy; they have reorganized a transnational marketplace for evasion across Eurasia, with Central Asian, Caucasian, Turkish, and Chinese intermediaries rerouting goods, energy, and payments while adapting to enforcement measures.

By 2025, according to Russian officials, 54% of trade was conducted in roubles and 31% in BRICS currencies, with crypto supporting flows. The EU's 20th sanctions package has banned digital rouble and rouble-linked stablecoin transactions by end of 2026 — but the evasion ecosystem has already metastasized beyond any single enforcement instrument. Crypto platforms, regional banks, and logistics hubs in Kyrgyzstan and Kazakhstan now serve as laboratories for sanctions evasion.

The energy dimension is critical: Russia's deputy prime minister Alexander Novak announced that Kazakh crude previously supplied to Germany via the North Druzhba pipeline would be diverted to other routes. This affects the PCK Schwedt refinery, which supplies one-sixth of Germany's oil and fuels 90% of vehicles in Berlin and Brandenburg, as well as jet aviation. German pump prices have reached 2.43 euros per litre, and Lufthansa has cancelled 20,000 flights. The sanctions architecture designed to punish Russia is generating second-order effects that impose costs on European consumers while increasing Moscow's leverage over transit states.
sections.whyItMatters: The trap for the West is that sanctions enforcement is now caught in a recursive loop: tighter enforcement pushes more Russian trade through the evasion ecosystem, strengthening the very intermediaries who profit from the architecture. The decisive move would be to shift from sanctions-as-punishment to sanctions-as-shaping — targeting the evasion nodes that enable the war economy rather than broadly punishing Russian economic activity. But this would require coordination with China, India, and Turkey that current diplomatic conditions do not permit.

sections.heading: Malacca Looms: The Next Chokepoint in Great Power Competition
sections.body:
While global attention focuses on Hormuz, a quieter development has taken shape in Southeast Asia. On April 14, the US and Indonesia announced a 'major defence cooperation partnership,' with Washington seeking wider access to Indonesian airspace. The move matters because Indonesia's archipelago sits astride the Strait of Malacca — an even narrower chokepoint than Hormuz, just 2.8km wide at the Phillips Channel, through which 24% of global seaborne trade and 45% of seaborne oil flows.

Both the US and China have been expanding military presence around the strait. The US has done so through base access and naval deployments; China through its port network and naval buildup. The Andaman and Nicobar Islands provide India with strategic presence at the strait's western approaches. Southeast Asia is becoming explicitly tied into great power competition, with the Strait of Malacca at its center.

China understands this exposure better than anyone. Between 75% and 80% of China's imported oil still passes through Malacca — the 'Malacca dilemma' identified by Hu Jintao in 2003. Beijing has invested in alternatives: the Myanmar pipeline (440,000 barrels/day), Gwadar Port via the China-Pakistan Economic Corridor, and Central Asian pipelines. But none match the scale of Malacca flows. The strategic implication is clear: in any Taiwan Strait crisis, the US would possess the same structural leverage over China's energy imports that Iran now wields over the global economy.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for China is accelerating pipeline and port infrastructure that reduces Malacca dependency — but the geography is unforgiving. The trap for the US is overconfidence: assuming Malacca can be held without consequence ignores that China has spent two decades building redundancy. The next chokepoint war will not be fought where the US expects.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals: Congo's Paramilitary Mining Guard
sections.body:
The Democratic Republic of Congo has created a $100 million US- and UAE-backed paramilitary mining guard to secure its critical mineral operations. The move represents a structural shift in how great powers secure access to cobalt, copper, and rare earths — from market-based extraction to militarized supply chain protection.

Congo produces approximately 70% of the world's cobalt, essential for electric vehicle batteries and defense applications. The creation of a dedicated paramilitary force signals that Western powers now treat critical mineral security as a military matter comparable to oil infrastructure protection. The UAE's involvement reflects Gulf states' diversification strategies — moving from hydrocarbon exporters to critical mineral investors.

This development intersects with the broader energy transition geopolitics. As oil prices spike due to Hormuz, the economic case for electric vehicles strengthens — but EV supply chains depend on cobalt and lithium whose extraction is increasingly contested. The energy transition is not replacing geopolitical competition; it is shifting it from hydrocarbons to minerals, with the same chokepoint dynamics emerging around mining regions rather than shipping lanes.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for the US would be to build a coherent critical minerals security architecture that treats Congo, Indonesia, and other mineral-rich states as strategic assets requiring permanent protection. The trap is that militarizing mineral extraction accelerates Chinese and Russian efforts to build alternative supply chains, fragmenting global markets and raising costs for all participants.

sections.heading: The Renewable Security Dividend: Crisis Reveals Structural Advantage
sections.body:
The Iran war is sending shockwaves through energy, food, and finance systems, exposing deep structural vulnerabilities — but also revealing a stark divide between fossil fuel-dependent and renewable-powered economies. World Resources Institute analysts note that countries that invested in domestic renewable energy are now in a 'better position to withstand this current crisis,' while fossil fuel importers face 'precarious energy positions.'

China, after years of investment in EVs and renewable power, has reduced its exposure to imported fossil fuels. Pakistan's surge in solar capacity has cushioned gas supply disruptions. France and Spain, with less gas-dependent electricity, show more stable prices than peers. African nations with 30 days of fuel reserves versus the normal 90 days face acute vulnerability. The lesson emerging is that renewable energy provides not just environmental benefits but 'economic and security features — security of domestic supply and stability of costs.'

The cascade effects are severe. Nitrogen fertilizers depend heavily on natural gas, with supply chains running through the Gulf. Global urea prices have surged, with reduced fertilizer use likely translating into lower crop yields in the next planting cycle. For Africa, which imports 80% of its fertilizer, the timing is catastrophic — this is planting season. The WRI warns of multiple 'F' crises: fuel, food, and fiscal strain compounding existing debt vulnerabilities.

Energy shocks are also pushing households across Africa and South Asia back to charcoal and firewood, reversing years of progress toward cleaner LPG cooking. In Kenya's Kibera settlement, charcoal use is climbing as LPG becomes unaffordable. In India, families are reverting to firewood stoves after years of LPG adoption programs. The conservation impact is severe: increased deforestation, habitat pressure, and wildlife-human conflict as people go deeper into forests for fuel.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for vulnerable states is rapid renewable deployment not for climate reasons but for sovereignty — reducing exposure to chokepoint politics controlled by distant powers. The trap for fossil fuel exporters is that each crisis accelerates this transition, eroding the demand base that underpins their economies. The energy security architecture is being rebuilt in real time, and the winners will be those who controlled the flows and those who escaped dependency on them.

sections.heading: Russia-Iran Strategic Partnership: The St. Petersburg Signal
sections.body:
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi met Vladimir Putin in St. Petersburg as the US-Iran standoff continues. Putin praised Iranians as 'bravely and heroically fighting for their sovereignty' and said Russia would do 'everything possible' to bring peace to the Middle East. The meeting signals that Russia sees Iran's crisis as an opportunity to deepen strategic partnership while extracting concessions from Western powers desperate for any diplomatic off-ramp.

Russia and Iran sealed a 20-year strategic partnership agreement last year. Russia is building two new nuclear units at Bushehr and has offered to store Iran's enriched uranium as a way of defusing tensions — an offer the US has not taken up. Iran has supplied Russia with Shahed drones for use against Ukraine. The two states are not merely aligned against Washington; they have built a structural interdependence that links nuclear diplomacy in the Gulf to the war in Ukraine.

The diplomatic theater obscures a strategic reality: Russia benefits from prolonged Hormuz instability. Higher oil prices fund the war economy. European energy costs rise. Western attention and resources are diverted from Ukraine to the Gulf. Moscow's offer to 'mediate' is a play for influence, not a genuine path to resolution.
sections.whyItMatters: The trap for Washington is treating Russia as a potential mediator rather than a beneficiary of the crisis. The decisive move would be to separate the Iran nuclear question from Russia's broader strategic interests — but this would require acknowledging that Moscow and Tehran have aligned interests in Western overextension, not just shared anti-Americanism.

sections.heading: Türkiye Energy Angle: Transit State Under Pressure
sections.body:
Türkiye's position as an energy transit corridor is being stress-tested by the Hormuz crisis. While not directly dependent on Gulf oil — Türkiye receives significant volumes via the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline and Russian gas via TurkStream — the global price spike affects an import-dependent economy. More critically, Türkiye's role as a sanctions evasion node for Russian trade is expanding under the pressure of the dual blockade architecture.

Recent cases reveal that Turkish firms are facilitating large-scale re-exports of sanctioned goods to Russia. As Central Asian and Caucasian intermediaries route more trade through Türkiye, Ankara gains leverage over both Western sanctions enforcement and Russian energy needs. The structural temptation is to play both sides: maintaining NATO membership while profiting from evasion networks.

The Hormuz closure also raises questions about Türkiye's own chokepoint potential. The Turkish Straits — Bosphorus and Dardanelles — connect the Black Sea to the Mediterranean. Russia's Black Sea fleet and grain exports transit these waters. Under the Montreux Convention, Türkiye can restrict passage during wartime. The precedent Iran is setting — that chokepoints can be weaponized against great powers — creates a strategic option for Ankara that did not exist before February 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Türkiye would be to position itself as the indispensable transit state for any post-crisis energy architecture — offering Western powers reliable alternatives to Hormuz while extracting concessions from Russia for Black Sea access. The trap is overplaying the hand: if Ankara becomes too central to evasion networks, it invites sanctions; if it aligns too closely with Western enforcement, it loses Russian leverage.

bottomLine: The Hormuz standoff has exposed the central vulnerability of the post-1973 energy security architecture: the entire system was built on the assumption that chokepoints would never be weaponized by state actors against great powers. Iran has now demonstrated that a relatively minor military power can hold 20% of global oil flows hostage, and the US response — a reciprocal blockade — confirms that chokepoint coercion is now standard statecraft. The swing producer crown passing to Washington represents a structural power shift, but it does not solve the underlying problem: energy security in a world of weaponized chokepoints requires either overwhelming military control of every strait or a fundamental reordering of supply routes. Neither is achievable in the near term. The decisive strategic reality is that the energy transition is now being driven not by climate policy but by energy sovereignty — states that control domestic renewable supply will escape the chokepoint trap, while fossil fuel importers remain vulnerable to coercion. Russia wins from prolonged Hormuz instability. China faces the same structural exposure at Malacca. The Gulf states are collateral damage in a war they cannot end. The architecture is broken; the question is who builds the next one.
sources: AP News, Reuters, Al-Monitor, World Resources Institute, The Conversation, MoneyLife, MSN, Domain-B, Mining.com, US News, NPR, CNN, Axios, TASS, IRNA
